title: Enum.reduce with non-obvious accumulator replaced by explicit recursion
prompt: Refactor MyApp.LogParser.group_sessions/1 in test_fixtures/enum_reduce_awkward.ex into lib/my_app/log_parser.ex. The input uses Enum.reduce with a 3-element tuple accumulator (current_session, completed_sessions, last_timestamp) and awkward conditional logic to decide when to close a session. Rewrite as explicit tail-recursive functions: group_sessions/1 (public entry) calls group_sessions/4 with initial accumulators. group_sessions/4 has THREE function heads: empty list base case, same-session case (when timestamp is within 30 minutes of last), and new-session case (otherwise). The final head returns Enum.reverse(completed) to restore order. This is the canonical 'Enum.reduce is the wrong tool; explicit recursion is clearer' refactor.
tier_rationale: Requires recognizing WHEN Enum.reduce is worse than explicit recursion (multi-field accumulator with conditional transitions) AND writing the correct tail-recursive dispatch. Most refactor challenges go Enum→recursion, this one goes recursion→Enum — the opposite direction. Sonnet often sticks with reduce or picks chunk_while as an 'Enum solution' that hides the same complexity.
